summary: Apple's updated iPhone Developer Program License Agreement now requires that apps be originally written in Objective-C, C, C++, or JavaScript executed by WebKit, effectively banning cross-compilers like Adobe's Flash-to-iPhone compiler and MonoTouch. Gruber analyzes the precise language and its implications for various third-party development tools, noting that some like PhoneGap may survive while others like Corona SDK and possibly Unity3D are out of bounds. He observes that Adobe is in a particularly dire position since the iPhone compiler is the flagship feature of the nearly-complete Flash CS5. Gruber argues Apple's broader intent is to prevent any third party from building a meta-platform on top of Cocoa Touch, leaving developers with exactly two sanctioned platforms: Cocoa Touch for native apps and the web.
key_points: The new section 3.3.1 requires apps be 'originally written' in approved languages and bans intermediary translation layers, effectively killing Flash-to-iPhone and MonoTouch
The impact on tools like PhoneGap, Titanium, and Unity3D is ambiguous, with each occupying a different gray area depending on how they produce final binaries
Adobe is in serious trouble since the iPhone compiler is the flagship feature of the about-to-ship Flash CS5
Apple's strategic goal is preventing any third party from defining a meta-framework for native iPhone development — only Cocoa Touch and the web are permitted platforms
Apple can likely enforce this by inspecting app bundle contents, which reveal telltale signs of cross-compilation
money_quotes: Applications must be originally written in Objective-C, C, C++, or JavaScript as executed by the iPhone OS WebKit engine.
It could hardly be more clear if they singled out Flash CS5 by name.
Wonder what Adobe does now? CS5 is thisclose to release and the iPhone compiler is the flagship feature in this version of Flash. They're pretty much royally fucked.
What Apple doesn't want — and as we see now, is not going to allow — is for anyone other than Apple to define the framework for native iPhone apps.
The iPhone OS supports two software platforms: Cocoa Touch and the web. Apple isn't going to let anyone else build a meta-platform on top of Cocoa Touch.
If you're going to write a native iPhone app, then you need to target our platform; if you want to do something else, then target the iPhone with an optimized web app.
key_insight: Apple's new developer agreement language is designed not to single out Flash specifically but to establish that no third party will be permitted to build a meta-platform on top of Cocoa Touch, leaving only Apple's native SDK and the open web as sanctioned development paths.
